turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Barrett has the opinion of the Court in case 21-432, Arellano versus McDonough.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: The Federal Government offers monetary benefits to any veteran who suffers from a disability connected to the military service. To receive these payments, a veteran must file a claim with the Department of Veteran Affairs. Once the VA receives the claim, it assigns an effective date that establishes when payments began. Effective dates are set by statute. The default role is that the effective date of an award cannot be earlier than the day on which the VA receives the veteran's application for benefits. But the statute specifies 16 exceptions to the default role, one of which, subsection (b)(1), is relevant here. If the VA receives an application within one year of the veteran's discharge from service, the effective date of the veteran's award is the day after the veteran's discharge. Where applicable, this exception allows a veteran up to one year of retroactive benefits. The petitioner in this case served in the Navy from 1977 to 1981, but did not apply for disability compensation until almost 30 years later. The VA applied the default rule to the petitioner's claim, which meant that his benefit payments commenced in 2011 on the day after he applied to the VA. The petitioner disagreed with that effective date and appealed the VA's decision. He alleged that his disability had prevented him from applying for benefits any sooner, and urged the VA to extend the one year grace period and (b)(1)'s exception through the doctrine of equitable tolling. That doctrine would effectively suspend (b)(1)'s time limit. If it were available, the petitioner's award could be effective as of the day after his discharge from the Navy in 1981, which would entitle him to 30 years of retroactive benefits payments. The courts below rejected the petitioner's argument and so do we. The statutory provision containing the default effective dates states that the rule applies unless specifically provided otherwise in this chapter. This language indicates that Congress enumerated an exhaustive list of exceptions with each confined to its specific terms. The (b)(1) exception allows up to one year of retroactive benefits payment but only if the application is filed within one year of discharge. Extending this grace period by equitably tolling it would depart from the terms that Congress specifically provided. We therefore hold that the day after discharge exception is not subject to equitable tolling and affirmed the judgment of the federal circuit. The decision of the court is unanimous.
